---
title: "One File, Two Outputs: Syncing Your Quarto About Page with Your GitHub Profile README"
author:
  - name: Guillaume Gilles
  - name: Claude Sonnet 4.6
    url: https://www.anthropic.com/claude
    roles:
      - implementation
      - writing - review & editing
abstract: |
  A deep dive into how Quarto's multi-format rendering pipeline, Pandoc's
  format family resolution, and a carefully guarded post-render Bash script
  can keep a single `about.qmd` in sync with your GitHub profile README —
  automatically, on every build, with zero duplication.
date: 2026-03-24
date-modified: 2026-04-01
categories: [Quarto, GitHub, Dev Tools, POSSE, Bash]
---

## The Problem

If you maintain a personal website **and** a [GitHub profile README](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/setting-up-and-managing-your-github-profile/customizing-your-profile/managing-your-profile-readme),
you know the drift problem: update your bio in one place, forget the other.
Add a certification here, miss it there. Two files, two truths, one inevitable inconsistency.

My website is built with [Quarto](https://quarto.org). The about page uses the
[Trestles template](https://quarto.org/docs/websites/website-about.html#templates) —
a two-column hero layout with a circular profile image, social links in a sidebar,
and rich HTML features (callout blocks, column divs, iframes) that simply don't
translate to GitHub-Flavored Markdown (GFM). So the two versions diverged.

The fix: a single `about.qmd` that renders to *both* HTML and GFM, with a
post-render Bash script that diffs and pushes the README to the profile repo on
every build. One edit, two outputs, zero drift.

## How Quarto's Multi-Format Pipeline Works

Before writing any code, it helps to understand what actually happens inside
`quarto render`.

Quarto is built on top of [Pandoc](https://pandoc.org/). When you write a `.qmd`
file, Quarto first executes any code cells (via Knitr or Jupyter), then passes
the resulting Markdown to Pandoc for conversion. Pandoc is what handles the
final format — HTML, PDF, GFM, etc.

### Format families and `.content-visible`

The `.content-visible when-format="..."` div syntax uses Quarto's **format
family** concept, not the raw Pandoc output format. The mapping matters:

| `--to` argument | Format family | `.content-visible` value |
|---|---|---|
| `html` | `html` | `"html"` ✅ |
| `gfm` | `markdown` | `"markdown"` ✅ |
| `gfm` | ~~`gfm`~~ | `"gfm"` ❌ |
| `pdf` | `latex` | `"latex"` ✅ |
| `commonmark` | `markdown` | `"markdown"` ✅ |

GFM is a *dialect* of Markdown processed by the `commonmark` Pandoc writer.
Quarto's format resolution maps it to the `markdown` family — so
`when-format="gfm"` silently fails, always evaluating to `false`.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Verify the format family

Run `quarto render about.qmd --to gfm --verbose` and look for the
`format:` line in the output. It will show `markdown`, not `gfm`.
:::

### The `freeze` cache and multi-format renders

If your project uses `freeze: auto` in `_quarto.yml`, Quarto caches code
execution results in `_freeze/`. When you render `about.qmd` again with
`--to gfm`, Quarto re-uses the frozen execution cache, so the script
runs quickly even with expensive computations in the document.

One subtlety: the freeze hash is keyed on the *source content*, not the
output format. Two renders of the same file to different formats share the
same execution cache, which is correct — the code output shouldn't change
between HTML and GFM.

## Structuring `about.qmd`

The file has three content zones:

### Zone 1 — HTML-only (Quarto website)

Wrap everything that uses Quarto-specific HTML features inside
`.content-visible when-format="html"`. This includes:

- The Trestles hero section (`#hero-heading` anchor required by Quarto)
- Multi-column divs (`.columns`, `.column`)
- Callout blocks (`::: {.callout-*}`)
- Iframes, Bootstrap cards, and any raw HTML

```{.markdown}
::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}

::: {#hero-heading}
# Your headline here

> Your tagline — one sharp sentence.
:::

::::: {.columns}
:::: {.column width="60%"}
Your main content here.
::::
:::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.callout-tip}
## Sidebar note
Something that complements the main text.
:::
::::
:::::

:::
```

The `#hero-heading` id is **required** by Quarto's Trestles template. Without
it, the template does not know where to inject the profile image and social
links sidebar — your page renders as plain text.

### Zone 2 — GFM-only (GitHub profile)

GitHub renders a special README from the repo `your-username/your-username`.
It supports a useful subset of HTML — `<div align="center">`, `<img>`,
`<p>` — but not Quarto divs, callouts, or the `:::` fencing syntax.

The GFM zone typically contains: a centered header with a profile image,
badge links (shields.io), a platform table, and a comment block warning
against direct edits:

```{.markdown}
::: {.content-visible when-format="markdown"}

<!-- ⚠️ AUTO-GENERATED — do not edit directly. -->
<!-- Source: https://github.com/your-username/your-username.github.io -->

<div align="center">

<img src="https://yoursite.dev/assets/profile.png"
     width="150" alt="Your Name" style="border-radius: 50%;" />

### Your Name

**Your one-liner.**

[![Website](https://img.shields.io/badge/Website-yoursite.dev-blue)](https://yoursite.dev)

</div>

---

| Platform | Profile | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **HackTheBox** | [username](https://app.hackthebox.com/profile/...) | Pentesting |
| **TryHackMe** | [username](https://tryhackme.com/p/...) | Cyber defense |

:::
```

The `<!-- AUTO-GENERATED -->` comment is important. Once you automate this,
someone (including future you) *will* try to edit the GitHub file directly.
The comment makes the source of truth explicit.

### Zone 3 — Shared content

Everything outside `.content-visible` blocks renders in **both** formats.
This is the content worth maintaining: your actual bio, experience, education,
tech stack. Keep the shared zone as format-neutral as possible — standard
Markdown tables, bold/italic, headings, and standard links all work in both HTML
and GFM.

```{.markdown}
<!-- No wrapper — renders in both HTML and GFM -->

I work at the intersection of data and security...

## 🛠️ Tech Stack

| Language | Use |
|---|---|
| Python | Data pipelines, scripting |
| R | Statistical analysis |

## 💼 Experience

- **Your Org** — Your role (N+ years)

## 🎓 Education

- **YYYY** — Your degree / certification
```

## The Post-Render Script — Deep Dive

The script at `scripts/update-profile-readme.sh` runs after every
`quarto render`. Here it is in full, with each section explained:

```{.bash filename="scripts/update-profile-readme.sh"}
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# post-render script: generate a GitHub profile README from about.qmd
# and push it to the your-username/your-username repo.
set -euo pipefail
```

`set -euo pipefail` is defensive scripting:

- `-e` — exit immediately if any command fails
- `-u` — treat unset variables as errors (catches typos like `$TMDIR`)
- `-o pipefail` — a pipeline fails if *any* command in it fails, not just
  the last one

```{.bash}
if [ "${PROFILE_README_RUNNING:-}" = "1" ]; then
  exit 0
fi
export PROFILE_README_RUNNING=1
```

### The recursion guard

This is the most non-obvious part of the setup and the first thing that will
silently break without explanation if you remove it.

Here is exactly what happens without the guard:

1. You run `quarto render` on your project.
2. The full site renders successfully.
3. The post-render hook fires and calls `quarto render about.qmd --to gfm`.
4. Quarto detects it is inside a project directory (`_quarto.yml` exists).
5. Quarto treats this as a *project* render, re-renders the whole site.
6. The post-render hook fires again. Go to step 3. Infinite loop.

The guard sets `PROFILE_README_RUNNING=1` before the first nested render.
When Quarto fires the hook the second time, the variable is already set,
the script exits immediately at line 2, and the loop terminates.

The `:-` in `${PROFILE_README_RUNNING:-}` is a Bash default-value expansion:
if the variable is unset, it expands to an empty string rather than triggering
`-u`'s "unset variable" error.

```{.bash}
REPO="your-username/your-username"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
TMPDIR="$(mktemp -d)"

cleanup() { rm -rf "$TMPDIR"; }
trap cleanup EXIT
```

`mktemp -d` creates a unique temporary directory under `/tmp`. The `trap cleanup EXIT`
ensures it is deleted regardless of how the script exits — normal completion,
error, or signal. Without the trap, a failed render leaves stale directories in `/tmp`.

`SCRIPT_DIR` is resolved with `cd && pwd` rather than `dirname "$0"` alone
because `$0` can be a relative path. The `cd` + `pwd` combination always
returns an absolute path, which is necessary when Quarto's working directory
during a post-render hook may differ from the project root.

```{.bash}
quarto render "$PROJECT_DIR/about.qmd" --to gfm \
  --output README.md --output-dir "$TMPDIR" 2>/dev/null

if [ ! -f "$TMPDIR/README.md" ]; then
  echo "❌ GFM render failed — README.md not produced."
  exit 1
fi
```

We render `about.qmd` with `--to gfm` to produce GFM Markdown. The
`--output README.md` flag names the output file (instead of `about.md`),
and `--output-dir "$TMPDIR"` places it in the temp directory. The `2>/dev/null`
suppresses Quarto's informational stderr output to keep the post-render log clean.

The existence check after the render is important: `set -e` catches non-zero
exit codes, but `quarto render` can exit `0` while still failing to produce
output in certain error conditions.

### Cleaning up Quarto's GFM artifacts

```{.bash}
# 1. Remove the auto-generated "# About" title (first 2 lines)
sed -i '1{/^# About$/d}' "$TMPDIR/README.md"
sed -i '1{/^$/d}' "$TMPDIR/README.md"

# 2. Fix shields.io badge URLs where Quarto appended .png
sed -i 's/logoColor=fff\.png/logoColor=fff/g' "$TMPDIR/README.md"
```

Two artifacts need fixing:

**Title injection.** Quarto reads the YAML `title: "About"` field and injects
`# About` as the first line of the GFM output, followed by a blank line. The
two `sed` commands delete that first line only if it matches `# About`, then
delete the now-leading blank line if one remains.

**Image extension appending.** Quarto's GFM writer applies the project-level
`default-image-extension` setting (which defaults to empty, then falls back to
`.png` in some versions) to every URL it identifies as an image path. Shields.io
badge URLs end in `...&logoColor=fff` — Quarto misidentifies the last segment as
an image path and appends `.png`, producing `...&logoColor=fff.png`, which
breaks the badge. The `sed` substitution strips the spurious `.png`.

This bug is specific to shields.io URL structure where the last query parameter
ends in a three-letter hex color code that Quarto's heuristic mistakes for a file
extension. A more robust fix would set `default-image-extension: ""` in `_quarto.yml`,
but the `sed` approach is simpler and requires no project-wide configuration change.

### Idempotent push — only if changed

```{.bash}
if ! git clone --depth 1 "git@github.com:${REPO}.git" \
     "$TMPDIR/profile-repo" 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "⚠️  Could not clone $REPO — skipping push."
  trap - EXIT   # prevent cleanup so you can inspect the file
  exit 0
fi

if diff -q "$TMPDIR/README.md" \
          "$TMPDIR/profile-repo/README.md" &>/dev/null; then
  echo "✅ Profile README is already up-to-date — nothing to push."
  exit 0
fi

cp "$TMPDIR/README.md" "$TMPDIR/profile-repo/README.md"
cd "$TMPDIR/profile-repo"
git add README.md
git commit -m "chore: sync profile README from yoursite.dev

Auto-generated from about.qmd by post-render script."
git push

echo "🚀 Profile README pushed to github.com/${REPO}"
```

`--depth 1` clones only the most recent commit (a *shallow clone*), which is
significantly faster than a full clone for a profile repo that may have hundreds
of commits. We only need the current `README.md` for the diff, not the full history.

The `diff -q` check makes the push **idempotent**: if nothing changed in
`about.qmd` since the last render, no commit is made. This avoids polluting the
profile repo's commit history with empty "no change" syncs on every build.

If cloning fails (no SSH key, network issue, wrong repo name), the script
degrades gracefully: it disables the cleanup trap so the generated `README.md`
survives in `/tmp` for manual inspection, then exits with code `0` so the
overall `quarto render` does not fail.

## Wiring It Into `_quarto.yml`

```{.yaml filename="_quarto.yml"}
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
  post-render:
    - scripts/update-profile-readme.sh
```

Post-render scripts run in the project root directory after all formats have
been rendered. Multiple scripts can be listed — they execute in order.

Make the script executable before the first render:

```{.bash}
chmod +x scripts/update-profile-readme.sh
```

## Authentication: SSH vs. HTTPS

The script uses `git@github.com:` (SSH). This requires:

1. An SSH key pair on the machine running `quarto render`
2. The public key added to GitHub under **Settings → SSH and GPG keys**

If you prefer HTTPS, replace `git@github.com:${REPO}.git` with
`https://github.com/${REPO}.git` and configure a credential helper or a
[personal access token](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/keeping-your-account-and-data-secure/managing-your-personal-access-tokens)
via `git config credential.helper store`.

For CI/CD (GitHub Actions), use a deploy key or `GITHUB_TOKEN` with
appropriate repo permissions:

```{.yaml filename=".github/workflows/publish.yml"}
- name: Render and publish
  env:
    GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
  run: |
    git config --global user.email "ci@yoursite.dev"
    git config --global user.name "CI Bot"
    quarto render
```

## Debugging Tips

**The README is not being updated.** Run the script manually with `bash -x`:

```{.bash}
bash -x scripts/update-profile-readme.sh
```

`-x` prints each command before executing it, making it easy to spot where
the script diverges from expectations.

**Shields.io badges show broken images.** Check for unexpected `.png` suffixes
in the generated file:

```{.bash}
grep "\.png" /tmp/$(ls -t /tmp | head -1)/README.md
```

Add additional `sed` rules for any other URL patterns Quarto mangles.

**The recursion guard triggers unexpectedly.** If `PROFILE_README_RUNNING` is
set in your shell environment from a previous failed run, new renders will
silently skip the README sync. Unset it:

```{.bash}
unset PROFILE_README_RUNNING
```

## The Result

Every `quarto render` now does double duty:

1. **Website** → `docs/about.html` — full Trestles layout with the profile image
   sidebar, column divs, callout blocks, and social links
2. **GitHub profile README** → auto-pushed to `your-username/your-username` with
   the centered GFM header, badge row, and all shared bio content

One source file. Two outputs. Zero drift. This is the [POSSE](https://indieweb.org/POSSE)
principle (*Publish on Own Site, Syndicate Elsewhere*) applied at the build-system level —
your website is the canonical source; GitHub is one of its syndication targets.
